34: elelythen ho huios tou anthropou esthion kai pinon — the Son of Man has come eating and drinking — the Son of Man self-reference in the social context of table-fellowship is Luke's Christological signature: the one with Danielic universal authority is present at dinner with sinners and tax collectors. The Pharisees' critique (phagos kai oinopotes, philos telonon kai hamartolon = a glutton and a drunkard, friend of tax collectors and sinners) is the social observation; Jesus turns it into the theological point: Wisdom is justified by all her children (v.35).
1: epeidē eplērōsen panta ta rhēmata autou eis tas akoas tou laou — when he had completed all his sayings in the hearing of the people. Luke’s transitional verse uses plērōsen (fulfilled, completed) — the same root as plēroō (to fulfill scriptural promises); the Sermon is “completed” as a formal discourse. Eis tas akoas tou laou (into the hearing of the people) — Luke emphasizes the public nature of the teaching; the word that has gone out to the crowd now leads to an action that demonstrates the same word’s power. The transition to Capernaum (Kapharnaum) grounds the following miracle in the teaching’s context.
2: hekatontarchou de tinos doulos kakōs echōn ēmellen teleutagn — a centurion’s servant, whom his master valued highly, was ill and about to die. Hekatontarchos (centurion, commander of one hundred) — a Roman military officer stationed in Capernaum, possibly attached to Herod Antipas’s forces. Doulos (slave/servant) — the word for a slave; contrast pais (v.7, child/boy) used by the centurion himself. The use of doulos by the narrator and pais by the centurion may indicate affection (pais as “my boy”) or a different tradition. Entimos (valued, precious, honored) indicates the centurion’s unusual care for a slave.
3: akousas peri tou Iēsou apesteilen pros auton presbyterous tōn Ioudaiōn — having heard about Jesus he sent elders of the Jews to him. The centurion’s intermediary approach through Jewish elders is socially sophisticated: a Gentile military officer sends the community’s own leaders as advocates, acknowledging Jewish religious authority. Presbyterous tōn Ioudaiōn (elders of the Jews) are the local synagogue leadership, not the Jerusalem Sanhedrin. The request (erōtōn auton hopōs elthōn diasōsē — asking him to come and heal) is a formal petition through proper social channels.
4: hoi de paragenomenoi pros ton Iēsoun parekaloun auton spoudaiōs — when they came to Jesus they pleaded earnestly with him. Spoudaiōs (earnestly, urgently) — the adverb intensifies the advocacy; the elders are genuinely persuaded of the centurion’s deserving. The word axios (deserving, worthy — v.4) begins the remarkable sequence where the centurion and Jesus each contest who is worthy: the elders say he is worthy; the centurion says he is not worthy (hikanon, v.6, and axios, v.7). The contest of unworthiness is the narrative heart of the pericope.
5: agapai gar to ethnos hēmōn kai tēn synagōgēn autos ōkodomēsen hēmin — he loves our nation and has built our synagogue for us. The elders’ advocacy argues from the centurion’s deeds: agapai to ethnos (he loves our nation) and tēn synagōgēn autos ōkodomēsen (he built our synagogue). This Gentile officer has funded the local synagogue — an act of extraordinary religious patronage and social good will. The argument is pragmatic: he has done good to Israel and deserves reciprocal good from Israel’s God. But Jesus will honor him for something else entirely.
6: ho de Iēsous eporeueto syn autois. ēdē de autou ou makran apechontos apo tēs oikias epempsas philous ho hekatontarchos — Jesus went with them. He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends to say: Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. The mid-journey interruption by a second message is a narrative heightening device. Mē skyllou (don’t trouble yourself, stop being bothered — skylloō is “to flay, then to trouble, harass”) is a polite urgency. Ou gar hikanos eimi hina hypo tēn stegēn mou eiselthēs — the centurion’s self-assessment of unworthiness (hikanos = sufficient, adequate) is the opposite of the elders’ advocacy.
7: dio oude eme axion hegēsamēn elthein pros se: alla eipe logō kai iathētō ho pais mou — that is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word and my servant will be healed. Axion hegēsamēn (I judged myself worthy) — the centurion’s self-judgment contradicts the elders’ judgment (v.4 axios estin); he alone knows his own inadequacy. Alla eipe logō (but say a word, say it with a word) — the centurion reduces the request to its absolute minimum: a single spoken word from Jesus is sufficient. He has understood something about the nature of Jesus’s authority that the disciples have not yet articulated.
8: kai gar egō anthrōpos eimi hypo exousian tassomenos echōn hyp emauton stratiōtas — for I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. The military analogy is the centurion’s theological argument: he understands command authority by experience. A soldier under authority (hypo exousian) can exercise delegated power (exousia) over those under him; his word is effective not because of his personal power but because of the authority behind him. The centurion infers that Jesus has a corresponding relationship to a higher authority — one whose command is effective at any distance, through word alone.
9: akousas de tauta ho Iēsous ethaumasen auton — when Jesus heard this, he marveled at him. Ethaumasen (marveled, was amazed) — one of only two places in the Gospels where Jesus is said to marvel (the other: Mark 6:6, at Nazareth’s unbelief). Here he marvels at the centurion’s faith; there, at his own townspeople’s unbelief. Oude en tō Israēl tosautēn pistin heuron (I have not found such great faith even in Israel) — the comparison is remarkable: a Gentile officer who has never been taught the Torah has grasped the nature of Jesus’s authority more clearly than those within the covenant. Faith, for Luke, is not ethnic privilege.
10: kai hypostrepsantes eis ton oikon hoi pemphthentes heuron ton doulon hygiainonta — those who had been sent returned to the house and found the servant well. The healing is confirmed by the returned messengers, not witnessed by Jesus or any disciple. Hygiainonta (healthy, well, sound — the root of hygiene) is the complete recovery. The narrative structure mirrors the healing: a word spoken at a distance produces a change at a distance, confirmed by witnesses at the destination. Luke omits any explicit mention of the moment of healing, focusing instead on the discovery of the result — the word’s effectiveness proven by outcome.
11: egeneto en tē hexēs eporeuēto eis polin kaloumenēn Naïn — soon afterwards Jesus went to a town called Nain. En tē hexēs (on the next day, afterward — the transitional phrase) connects the centurion’s story to the widow’s son. Nain (Naïn, “pleasant” in Hebrew) is a town in southern Galilee, near Shunem — where Elisha raised the widow’s son (2 Kgs 4:32-37). The geographical proximity to the Elisha miracle is likely deliberate; Luke will invite comparison. Hoi mathētai autou hikanoi kai ochlos polys (his disciples and a large crowd) — both a following and witnesses to what follows.
12: hōs de ēngisen tē pylē tēs poleōs, kai idou exekomizeto tethnēkōs — as he approached the town gate, a dead person was being carried out. The two processions — Jesus’s coming-in and the funeral procession going out — meet at the city gate (pylē), the threshold space between life-city and death-space. Tethnēkōs (dead person) and the three details of social tragedy: monogenēs (only son — the unique son, same word as John 3:16’s only-begotten), tē mētri (his mother), and chēra (widow). The triple tragedy — only son, bereaved mother, without a husband — exhausts the social support network. The crowd (ochlos hikanos) is the public mourning community.
13: kai idōn autēn ho kyrios esplanchnisthē ep autē kai eipen autē: mē klaie — when the Lord saw her, his heart went out to her and he said: Don’t cry. Esplanchnisthē (had compassion, was moved in the bowels) is Luke’s compassion verb — the visceral, gut-level response to human suffering (also 10:33 for the Samaritan; 15:20 for the father of the prodigal). Luke uses ho kyrios (the Lord) as a narrator’s title for Jesus (7:13; 10:1; 11:39; 13:15 etc.) — the LXX divine title used by the narrator as a Christological identifier. Mē klaie (stop weeping) is a command, not comfort; he intends to give her the reason to stop.
14: kai proselthōn hēpsato tēs sorou, hoi de bastaszontes estēsan — he went up and touched the bier, and those carrying it stood still. Hēpsato tēs sorou (touched the bier, the open stretcher for carrying a body) — contact with a corpse or bier renders a person ritually unclean (Num 19:11-13); Jesus initiates the contact deliberately, reversing the direction of contagion. Hoi de bastasontes estēsan (those carrying it stopped) — the bearers freeze at the touch, creating the arrested moment in which Jesus speaks. Neaniske, soi legō, egerthēti (young man, I say to you, rise) — the address is personal, intimate, authoritative; “I say to you” marks the word as direct divine command.
15: kai anekathisen ho nekros kai ērxato lalein — the dead man sat up and began to speak. Anekathisen (sat up, raised himself to sitting position) — the physical description is precise: the movement of one who was lying now rises from the bier. Ērxato lalein (began to speak) — the first action of the restored life is speech; the dead speak. Kai edōken auton tē mētri autou (and he gave him back to his mother) — the deliberate echo of 1 Kgs 17:23 (Elijah: “gave him to his mother”) and 2 Kgs 4:36 (Elisha: “gave him to his mother”). Jesus is aligned with but exceeds the Elijah-Elisha prophetic tradition.
16: elaben de phobos pantas kai edoxazon ton theon legontes hoti prophētēs megas ēgerthē en hēmin kai hoti epeskepsato ho theos ton laon autou — they were all filled with awe and praised God, saying: A great prophet has appeared among us; God has come to help his people. The double acclamation is the crowd’s theological interpretation: prophētēs megas ēgerthē (a great prophet has arisen) — the prophetic category places Jesus in the Elijah-Elisha succession; ēgerthē (has arisen, passive) is the same verb as the resurrection. Epeskepsato ho theos ton laon autou (God has visited his people) — the covenant-visitation language of Zechariah’s Benedictus (1:68); what Zechariah announced is now being enacted.
17: kai exēlthen ho logos houtos en holē tē Ioudaia peri autou kai pasē tē perichōrō — this news about Jesus spread throughout Judea and the surrounding country. Ho logos houtos (this word/report) — the same term (logos) used for the gospel proclamation; the report of a miraculous deed becomes the medium of wider proclamation. The spread from Galilee (Nain) throughout Judea and the surrounding regions prepares for John’s question (v.18): the news of Jesus has reached the prison in which John is held (3:20).
18: kai apēngeilan Iōannē hoi mathētai autou peri pantōn toutōn — John’s disciples told him about all these things. The connection between the Nain miracle and John’s question is explicit: John has received reports of Jesus’s activity and now asks the decisive messianic question. Proskalesamenos duo tinas tōn mathētōn autou ho Iōannēs (calling two of his disciples) — the formal dispatch of two messengers follows Deuteronomic legal procedure for testimony (Deut 19:15); John’s question is framed as a legal inquiry requiring a witnessed answer.
19: su ei ho erchomenos ē allon prosdokōmen — are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else? Ho erchomenos (the Coming One) is the messianic title from Psalm 118:26 (“Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD”) and Malachi 3:1 (“the Lord, whom you seek, will suddenly come to his temple”). John used the Coming One language in his own proclamation (3:16 — “one more powerful than I is coming”); he now asks whether Jesus is that figure or whether he should recalibrate his expectation. The question is not unbelief but eschatological discernment.
20: Iōannēs ho baptistēs apesteilen hēmas pros se legōn: su ei ho erchomenos ē allon prosdokōmen — John the Baptist sent us to you to ask: Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else? The repetition of John’s question by the messengers is a Lukan narrative technique of double confirmation (the messengers report the exact words). The formal identification of the sender — “John the Baptist” rather than simply “John” — signals the official character of the inquiry: this is John’s prophetic question, not a casual personal doubt.
21: en ekeinē tē hōra etherapeusen pollous apo nosōn kai mastegōn kai pneumatōn ponērōn kai typhlos pollois echarisato blepein — at that very time Jesus cured many who had diseases, sicknesses and evil spirits, and gave sight to many who were blind. Luke’s unique staging: Jesus performs miracles as John’s messengers watch. The answer to John’s question is not a verbal proposition but a demonstration. Mastegōn (scourges, plagues — literally whips; used metaphorically for chronic afflictions, also 8:29) indicates the severity of the conditions. The giving of sight to the blind is foregrounded because it is the first item in Jesus’s answer (v.22).
22: poreuphentes apangeilate Iōannē ha eidete kai ēkousate: typhloi anablepousin, chōloi peripatousin, leproi katharizontai kai kōphoi akouousin, nekroi egeirontai, ptōchoi euangelizontai — go back and report to John what you have seen and heard. The six miracle-categories are an Isaiah-mosaic: blind see (Isa 29:18; 35:5), lame walk (Isa 35:6), lepers cleansed (Isa 61?), deaf hear (Isa 35:5), dead raised (Isa 26:19), poor receive good news (Isa 61:1). The accumulation of Isaiah’s new-exodus signs in one answer confirms that Jesus is not merely a coming one but the eschatological deliverer of the servant songs. The dead raised and the poor evangelized are the climax — the first is the greatest miracle, the second is the most counter-cultural claim.
23: kai makarios estin hos ean mē skandalisthē en emoi — blessed is anyone who does not stumble on account of me. Skandalisthē (stumble, be offended — the skandalon is the trigger-stick of a trap, the stone of stumbling) — the blessing is for those who do not trip over the unexpected character of Jesus’s messiahship. The implicit warning is for John: the imprisoned prophet, who expected a fiery-axe Messiah (3:9, 17), must not be offended that the Coming One heals and proclaims rather than judges and burns. The beatitude is a gentle test.
24: kakamos tōn angelōn Iōannou ērxato legein pros tous ochlous peri Iōannou — after John’s messengers left, Jesus began to speak to the crowd about John. The timing is deliberate: Jesus defends John in John’s absence to the crowds who had questioned John’s integrity. Three rhetorical questions (ti exēlthate eis tēn erēmon theasasthai; — what did you go out to see?) use anaphora to build toward the climactic answer. The rhetorical questions are not mere oratory but a diagnostic of the crowds’ own inconsistency in rejecting John after flocking to him.
25: alla ti exēlthate idein; anthrōpon en malakoisa himatiois ēmphiesmenon — but what did you go out to see? A man dressed in fine clothes? Malakoisa himatiois (soft, luxurious garments) — contrasted with John’s camel-hair clothing (Mark 1:6). Hoi en himatismō endoxō kai tryphe hyparchontes en tois basileiois eisin (those in splendid clothing and luxury are in royal palaces) — the implicit contrast: John is the opposite of the courtier; his presence in the desert is by choice, his message not shaped by the need for royal favor. The crowd went out to a desert prophet, not a palace advisor.
26: alla ti exēlthate idein; prophētēn; nai, legō hymin, kai perissoteron prophētou — but what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and more than a prophet. The climactic answer: nai (yes) — John is indeed a prophet; kai perissoteron prophētou (and more than a prophet) — the “more than” (perissoteron, excess, surplus) signals a qualitative difference. All the OT prophets announced the coming day; John announces the coming person. He does not merely predict the Messiah — he is the Messiah’s immediate forerunner, stationed at the hinge between preparation and fulfillment.
27: houtos estin peri hou gegraptai: idou apostello ton angelon mou pro prosopou sou, hos kataskeuasei tēn hodon sou emprosthen sou — this is the one about whom it is written: I will send my messenger ahead of you who will prepare your way before you. The composite citation blends Malachi 3:1 (“I will send my messenger, who will prepare the way before me”) with Exodus 23:20 (“I will send an angel before you”). The pronoun shift in Malachi is significant: “before me” (in Malachi) becomes “before you” (the quotation addressed to Jesus) — identifying Jesus as the “me” of Malachi: YHWH coming to his temple.
28: legō hymin, meizōn en gennētois gynaikōn Iōannou oudeis estin: ho de mikroteros en tē basileia tou theou meizōn autou estin — I tell you, among those born of women no one is greater than John; yet the one who is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he. The superlative praise followed by the paradox: the greatest of the old order is surpassed by the least in the new. Mikroteros (least, smaller) — the comparative of mikros; the paradox is not about rank within the kingdom but about the eschatological privilege of kingdom membership. John stands at the threshold; those inside, however least, participate in the fulfilled reality he could only herald. This verse is a key statement of Lukan inaugurated eschatology.
29: kai pas ho laos akousas kai hoi telōnai edikaiōsan ton theon baptisthentes to baptisma Iōannou — all the people, even the tax collectors, acknowledged that God’s way was right, because they had been baptized by John. Luke’s parenthetical narrator-comment (vv.29-30) interprets the two groups’ responses to John. Edikaiōsan ton theon (declared God righteous, justified God) — the reverse of the judicial language: the people who were baptized pronounced that God was in the right in sending John and requiring repentance. The tax collectors are the paradigm case of the unexpected respondents to John’s message.
30: hoi de pharisaioi kai hoi nomikoi tēn boulēn tou theou ēthētēsan eis heautous mē baptisthentes hyp autou — but the Pharisees and the experts in the law rejected God’s purpose for themselves, because they had not been baptized by John. Tēn boulēn tou theou ēthētēsan (nullified/rejected God’s purpose/plan) — boulē (purpose, plan, counsel) is Luke’s term for the divine redemptive plan (Acts 2:23; 4:28; 20:27); to reject baptism is not merely personal religious choice but opposition to God’s overarching plan. Eis heautous (for themselves, against their own interest) — the rejection harms them, not the plan.
31: tini oun homoiōsō tous anthrōpous tēs geneas tautēs kai tini eisin homoioi — to what, then, can I compare the people of this generation? What are they like? Hē genea hautē (this generation) is a consistent term in Luke for the contemporary generation that has witnessed Jesus and John and refused both (11:29, 30, 31, 32, 50, 51; 17:25; 21:32). The comparison is a parabolic pronouncement of judgment shaped as a diagnostic: what is the character of a people who reject both John’s austerity and Jesus’s feasting?
32: homoioi eisin paidois tois en agorā kathēmenois kai prosfonousin allēlois ha legei: ēulēsamen hymin kai ouk ōrchēsasthe: ethrēnēsamen kai ouk eklausate — they are like children sitting in the marketplace calling out to each other: We played the flute and you did not dance; we sang a dirge and you did not cry. The children’s game-parable depicts sulky children who refuse to play either wedding or funeral. Ēulēsamen (we played the flute — the wedding music) and ethrēnēsamen (we sang a dirge — funeral lament) — the two basic communal responses to life’s transitions. The generation refuses both: no joy at the wedding of God’s kingdom, no sorrow at the funeral of their own spiritual condition.
33: elēlythen gar Iōannēs ho baptistēs mē esthiōn arton mēte pinōn oinon kai legete: daimonion echei — for John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say: He has a demon. The two rejections are diagnostic of the generation’s condition: John came with the ascetic intensity of a Nazirite and was accused of demonization. The refusal of food and wine (mē esthiōn arton mēte pinōn oinon) marks John’s prophetic separation; his critics explain this not as consecration but as disorder — daimonion echei (he has a demon).
35: kai edikaiōthē hē sophia apo pantōn tōn teknōn autēs — but wisdom is proved right by all her children. Hē sophia (wisdom, the divine plan embodied in both John and Jesus) is vindicated not by argument but by her children — those who have responded to John’s baptism and Jesus’s ministry. Luke’s version reads “all her children” (Matthew: “her deeds”); the children are the tax collectors and sinners who have responded, justifying God (v.29). The conclusion is ironic: the religious experts who should be wisdom’s advocates have rejected her; the disreputable have welcomed her.
36: ērōta de tis auton tōn pharisaiōn hina phagē met autou: kai eiselthōn eis ton oikon tou pharisaiou kateklithē — when one of the Pharisees invited Jesus to have dinner with him, he went to the Pharisee’s house and reclined at the table. Jesus accepts the dinner invitation of a Pharisee named Simon (v.40) — demonstrating that the love-your-enemies principle extends to eating with those who will challenge him. The dinner setting is a semi-public event; invited guests recline, but others could enter the dining room. The table is the arena of confrontation, hospitality, and forgiveness in this episode.
37: kai idou gynē hētis ēn en tē polei hamartōlos kai epignousa hoti katakeitai en tē oikia tou pharisaiou — a woman in that town who lived a sinful life learned that Jesus was eating at the Pharisee’s house. Gynē hamartōlos (a sinful woman) — the term designates social reputation rather than a specific sin; the town’s judgment of her is what matters for the narrative’s social dynamics. Epignousa (having learned, recognized — the prefix epi- intensifying the knowing) — her coming is deliberate and informed: she knows where Jesus is and goes. The alabaster jar of perfume (alabastron myrou) is an expensive luxury, her act an extravagant gift.
38: kai stasa opisō para tous podas autou klaiōusa, tois dakrysin ērxato brechein tous podas autou kai tais thrixin tēs kephalēs autēs exemassen — as she stood behind him at his feet weeping, she began to wet his feet with her tears, then wiped them with her hair, kissed them and poured perfume on them. The fourfold action (wet with tears, wipe with hair, kiss, pour perfume) is an escalating sequence of loving service. Using her hair to wipe his feet is a culturally transgressive act: a woman’s unbound hair in public was socially scandalous, but she uses her greatest social asset (her hair) as a towel for the teacher’s feet. The excess of her devotion corresponds to the excess of her forgiven debt.
39: idōn de ho pharisaios ho kalestas auton eipen en heautō legōn — when the Pharisee who had invited him saw this, he said to himself. Eipen en heautō (said within himself) — the Pharisee’s internal monologue is accessible to the reader and to Jesus, though not spoken aloud. Ei hē prophētēs ēn (if this man were a prophet) — the conditional is contrary-to-fact in the Pharisee’s mind: he has concluded Jesus is not a prophet because a prophet would know the woman’s sinful character and avoid her touch. The Pharisee’s logic assumes ritual purity requires physical distance from the impure.
40: kai apokritheis ho Iēsous eipen pros auton: Simōn, echō soi ti eipein — Jesus answered him: Simon, I have something to tell you. Jesus’s response to the unspoken thought (apokritheis — answered, though no question has been spoken aloud) is itself the answer to the Pharisee’s doubt: a true prophet does know — he reads the unspoken thought and addresses it. Didaskale, eipe (Teacher, speak) — Simon’s respectful form of address acknowledges Jesus as teacher even while questioning his prophetic status. The parable that follows is designed to lead Simon to judge rightly — and then to recognize that he has judged himself.
41: dyo chreopheiletat ēsan daneistē tini: ho heis ophile dēnaria pentakosia ho de heteros pentēkonta — two people owed money to a certain moneylender. One owed five hundred denarii, the other fifty. The parable’s debt-amounts are carefully calibrated: 500 denarii was roughly 500 days’ wages — almost a year and a half’s income; 50 denarii was a month and a half. Both are substantial debts impossible to repay quickly; the quantitative difference (10:1) is large enough to make the love-response proportional. The moneylender (daneistē) is not criticized; the parable is about gratitude, not usury.
42: mē echontōn de autōn apodounai amphotherois echarisato: tis oun autōn pleon agapēsei auton — neither of them had the money to pay him back, so he forgave the debts of both. Now which of them will love him more? Echarisato (he forgave, freely gave — charizomai shares the root of charis, grace) — the forgiveness is an act of grace, not a legal accommodation. The question Jesus asks Simon is designed to elicit self-condemnation: Simon will rightly identify the greater love as belonging to the greater debtor — and then discover that he himself has been playing the role of the smaller debtor who shows less love.
43: apokritheis Simōn eipen: hypolambano hoti hō to pleion echarisato — Simon replied: I suppose the one who had the bigger debt forgiven. Hypolambano (I suppose, I assume — with some reluctance in the formulation) — Simon senses the trap but cannot avoid answering honestly; the parable’s logic is inescapable. Orthōs ekrinas (you have judged rightly) — Jesus affirms the judgment; the word krinas (judged) will now be turned back on Simon: he has just judged the case, and the case is his own.
44: kai strapheis pros tēn gynaika tō Simōni ephē: blepeis tautēn tēn gynaika — turning toward the woman he said to Simon: Do you see this woman? The physical turn toward the woman while speaking to Simon is a deliberate rhetorical gesture: Jesus places the woman in Simon’s line of sight for a new kind of seeing. Blepeis tautēn (do you see this woman?) — Simon has seen the woman as a hamarotlos (sinner); Jesus asks him to see a person with a story of love and forgiveness. The three comparisons (no water / tears, no kiss / kissing, no oil / perfume) systematically expose Simon’s failure of hospitality.
45: philēma moi ouk edōkas: hautē de aph hēs eisēlthon ou dieleipen kataphilousa mou tous podas — you did not give me a kiss, but this woman, from the time I entered, has not stopped kissing my feet. The contrast is between the minimal courtesy (philēma, greeting kiss) that Simon withheld and the continuous devotion (ou dieleipen kataphilousa, she has not stopped kissing) that the woman has given from the moment of entry. Kataphilousa (kissing fervently, with intensity — the prefix kata- intensifying) — the woman’s devotion is not a single act but a sustained expression of love. The contrast exposes the relative magnitude of each one’s sense of their own debt.
46: elaiō tēn kephalēn mou ouk ēleipsas: hautē de myrou tous podas mou ēleipsen — you did not put oil on my head, but she has poured perfume on my feet. The third comparison is the most emphatic: anointing the head with common oil was a simple act of hospitality; anointing the feet with costly perfume is an extravagant reversal. The woman has given the more precious gift to the less valued body part. The oil/perfume contrast and the head/feet contrast together demonstrate the inversion of values that forgiveness creates: the forgiven one pours out her most precious resource at the lowest point.
47: hou charin legō soi: apheōntai hai hamartiai autēs hai pollai, hoti ēgapēsen poly — therefore I tell you, her many sins have been forgiven — as her great love has shown. The causal direction is debated: does her love cause the forgiveness, or does the forgiveness cause the love? The parable settles it: the debtor loves because the debt has been forgiven — not the reverse. Apheōntai (have been forgiven, divine perfect passive — already accomplished) — the forgiveness precedes the actions; her love is the evidence of prior forgiveness, not its cause. Hō de oligon aphietai, oligon agapāi (but whoever has been forgiven little loves little) — the contrast implies Simon has a large debt but a small sense of it.
48: eipen de autē: apheōntai sou hai hamartiai — then Jesus said to her: Your sins are forgiven. The public declaration of forgiveness (apheōntai sou hai hamartiai) is the gospel in miniature: Jesus speaks the divine word of release. The perfect passive apheōntai (have been forgiven, are forgiven — both completed and present) places the forgiveness in both God’s past act and the woman’s present status. The declaration is public, before the table of people who know her reputation — the social rehabilitation of a forgiven person is as important as the spiritual reality.
49: kai ērxanto hoi synanakeimenoi legein en heautois: tis houtos estin hos kai hamartias aphiēsin — the other guests began to say among themselves: Who is this who even forgives sins? The table-guests’ reaction echoes the scribes’ response to the paralytic’s healing (5:21): “Who can forgive sins but God alone?” The question tis houtos estin (who is this?) is the reader’s question throughout Luke — and the answer is precisely what the question implies: only God forgives sins; therefore Jesus is doing what only God does.
50: eipen de pros tēn gynaika: hē pistis sou sesōken se; poreuou eis eirēnēn — Jesus said to the woman: Your faith has saved you; go in peace. Hē pistis sou sesōken se (your faith has saved you) — the same formula Jesus uses for other healing-salvation events (8:48; 17:19; 18:42). Sōzō (save) encompasses both physical healing and spiritual salvation in Luke; the woman’s “saving” is the comprehensive restoration of her person before God and in the community. Poreuou eis eirēnēn (go into peace, go in peace) — the Hebrew shalom farewell; the peace is not merely the absence of conflict but the positive wholeness of restored relationship with God.
